output_text:
Revised the section: tightened claims, sharpened the contribution framing, replaced soft transitions with concrete signposting, and foregrounded the phase-transition result.

---

Multi-agent dialogue systems built on large language models are increasingly proposed as a substrate for autonomous task completion, yet their convergence behavior is understood almost exclusively in the homogeneous regime where every agent is sampled from the same base model. Park et al. showed that role-conditioned agents can sustain extended task-oriented exchanges; Zhang and Bose characterized the equilibria of two-agent inception-prompted dialogue under fixed-temperature decoding; follow-up work extends both analyses to mixed-role populations and to dialogues moderated by a critic agent. In every case, the analysis hinges on a shared prompt prefix and bounded decoding noise, and yields an O(1/sqrt(N)) total-variation convergence rate for the N-round joint trajectory to a stationary, role-consistent distribution.

This shared-prefix assumption is the binding one in practice. Deployments routinely pair a user-simulator drawn from one base model with an assistant drawn from another — for cost, capability, or safety reasons — and the existing theory says nothing about whether such pairs converge at all.

We close this gap. Let the user-agent be sampled from model M_U and the assistant-agent from M_A, and let delta denote the KL divergence between their next-token distributions on the shared role prompt. We instrument the dialogue with a per-round drift estimator d_t and measure its evolution over 10,000 simulated episodes from AgentBench-Lite. Three findings:

1. **A sharp phase transition.** d_t grows sublinearly for delta < 0.15 and superlinearly above it, with the transition concentrated near delta = 0.18. The effect is stable across three task families and survives insertion of the Chen-Iyer moderator.

2. **A matching theoretical prediction.** Replacing the single-prefix step in the Zhang-Bose contraction argument with a two-prefix coupling yields a closed-form critical delta* that agrees with the empirical transition to within 8%.

3. **The first convergence guarantee for heterogeneous-model dialogue,** together with an operational criterion — measure delta, compare to delta* — that tells a practitioner in advance whether a given M_U / M_A pairing will remain role-stable.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: I did not verify the cited works (Park et al., Zhang and Bose, Chen and Iyer, AgentBench-Lite) exist or that the technical claims (the O(1/sqrt(N)) prior rate, the 8% match, the delta = 0.18 transition) are accurate — I preserved them as given. If any are placeholders or misremembered, the revised text inherits the error.
